feedback.text: As an EU citizen, I welcome the ambition of the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) to address manipulative and unfair digital practices. I would like to highlight a set of related consumer harms that I believe fall squarely within its objectives. 1. Games as a Service: Unilateral takedowns and coerced spending Consumers today routinely purchase digital games only to see them unilaterally taken away when publishers discontinue these games. Current End User Licence Agreements often allow service withdrawal at any time for any or no reason, leaving consumers with nothing, even when they have spent substantial amounts. This practice undermines consumer rights for corporate profit, erasing older games and coercing players to buy newer titles. These practices weaponize artificial scarcity and exploit legal ambiguity over whether digital games are goods or services. The DFA should mandate enforceable endoflife plans for digital games. Publishers must provide a means for consumers to continue running products they purchased even after active support ends. Anything less leaves consumers perpetually hostage to industry dictates. This connects directly to manipulative monetization schemes. Ingame currencies are sold in deliberately awkward bundles, obscuring real costs and leaving unused credits that pressure further spending. Loot boxes and RNGbased purchases are gambling by another name and prey on vulnerable individuals. These gamblinglike mechanics should be banned outright, and all purchases should be direct, transparent currencytopurchase transactions. 2. Monopolistic intermediaries as de facto gatekeepers and censors Recently, Visa and Mastercard, who together control approximately 70% of global payment processing outside of China, pressured multiple online stores to remove thousands of entirely legal games. Platforms complied because losing these payment processors would be financially unsustainable. For the consumer, the effect is identical to unilateral publisher takedowns: lawful products vanish overnight. When private companies can dictate what lawful digital goods EU citizens may access, it is not a free market choice, it is censorship. The DFA should address how dominant intermediaries can undermine consumer rights and effectively censor digital marketplaces. 3. Erosion of consumer freedom through external pressure We are also witnessing the ripple effects of new UK ageverification measures, which compel invasive face scans or government ID checks to access certain online content. Although the UK is no longer an EU member state, companies are already signalling they intend to implement these measures EUwide. This would force EU citizens to surrender highly sensitive personal data to third parties, in clear conflict with the principles of the GDPR and the EUs commitment to privacy. It would be deeply concerning if the EUs own citizens lost access to legal content or were forced to undergo invasive data collection because of regulations from outside the Union. This is a direct threat to consumer autonomy and EU sovereignty over its digital space. Companies should not be allowed to impose surveillance measures in the EU simply because it is operationally easier. In summary: Consumers must have enforceable rights to continue accessing the digital goods they purchase. Manipulative monetization tactics and addictive design practices like RNGbased purchases should be banned. The Act should address how dominant intermediaries and external regulatory pressures erode consumer choice, privacy, and EU sovereignty in the digital space. I believe these issues are deeply connected and fall within the Digital Fairness Acts mandate to protect consumers from unfair practices online. The DFA has an opportunity and an obligation to send a clear message: EU citizens are not secondclass citizens whose access to lawful goods can be revoked at the whim of industry actors, gatekeepers, or external governments.
